methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $40M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Senra Systems is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company founded by Jordan Black and Ben Shanahan that has raised $40M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a sector universe of 9 private robotics companies and 1 public comparable (277810.KQ), though narrow peer-stage matching constraints limit direct application of median multiples.

The stage-progression method is applicable and primary. Using the $40M total raised as a valuation proxy, a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A-to-next-stage progression implies a valuation of $80M. The peer-median approach is not applicable: while 7 same-sector robotics companies exist (including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity), only 2 trade at adjacent funding stages and none meet minimum threshold criteria (≥3 datapoints) for a reliable same-stage or similar-tier cohort. Applying an all-sector median would introduce undue noise given stage and capital-intensity dispersion within robotics.

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $68M (low) to $92M (high), with a median of $80M. This range reflects conservative application of stage-progression mechanics. Key uncertainties include traction and unit economics relative to the identified peer set, the relevance of public comparables given robotics subsector variance, and post-Series A capital requirements. The single-method derivation underscores the need for additional financial and operational data to tighten the range.
